next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

The amber light on Marisol's downgraded passcard blinked twice before the custody officer on the car door lost patience and slapped his own badge over the plate. The lock released with a flat metal cough. Heat pushed out of the seam hard enough to salt the inside of her nose. Somebody had already vomited. Bleach sat over it and did not win.

The announcement was still repeating in the car's clipped synthetic English and Spanish, the same four lines cycling over the ceiling speakers until the words stopped meaning anything except that they were happening to bodies: full biometric reconciliation, aisle compliance, prepare tags, remove nothing from your berth. The floor thrummed under her boots with the long steady vibration of southbound speed. The car swayed left, then right, and the portable scan units answered with little irritated chirps every time their handlers had to reset their stance.

This was Teresa's answer to the audit discrepancies. Scan the whole car again. Wash the paperwork through a second machine and call it truth.

Marisol stepped in sideways because a line of knees already clogged the first third of the aisle. The air was body-hot, wet with old breath and overheated plastic. Children had been pulled down from upper bunks too early and were now drooping against mothers' thighs or staring at the scanners with the glassy patience of the over-tired. Men in wrist restraints sat along the right side under the windows, tags hanging from their necks. Families and overflow detainees were stacked into fold-down bunks on the left, three high, blankets k...
open_hooks_to_carry: Publicly strip Marisol's authority in ways that change future logistics.
Tie worker obedience to housing deductions and summer earnings.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...pended on people like her turning mess into process before anybody outside the corridor had to look hard.

Marisol folded the pages once, careful along the crease so the grease pencil would not smear too badly, and slid them inside the plastic sleeve behind her dead comm tablet. They were not hidden well, only hidden fast. Her body already knew the motion from years of tucking unofficial things inside official ones. She hated that knowledge too.

Outside, the marsh breathed its rotten-sweet breath through the broken screen. The mosquitoes kept coming. The train sat in the heat with its locks alive and its people awake inside it.

Marisol picked up the recorder again though the battery was dead, just to feel its weight. Then she rose from the waste drum, knees stiff, shirt damp, the salted water gone metallic at the back of her tongue. There was another round to make. There were children in hot bunks, an old man trying to faint politely, guards getting stupid with fatigue, tanks filling under the floor. All of that remained true.

So did the names.

For the first time since she had seen Gabriel's face in intake, Marisol understood that if she kept risking anything on this line, it could not be for the private return of one son out of hundreds. It had to be for the part the train was built to erase: the stubborn fact that each person inside it had arrived with a name, a habit, a wrong country on a tag, a child who chewed collars, a key that fit a real door somewhere. The machine could move bodies south by dawn. It could not have those things too, not if she kept hold of them.
state_deltas_required: Teresa's surprise audit, downgraded credentials, and worker fear around housing and overtime.
Marisol loses operational cover, Gabriel is moved to higher security, and Paloma quietly crosses into complicity.
